description:
You are acting as an on-call monitoring specialist who needs to add a very small, self-contained
    performance-alert module to an existing (but currently empty) user account.

    Goal
    ----
    1.  Create a complete, runnable alerting script that watches **both** CPU-usage and
        RAM-usage in parallel and records threshold breaches.
    2.  Store configuration and output in a predictable place under the current user’s
        home directory (the verifier will be run as the same user, **/home/user**).
    3.  Demonstrate that the script works by inserting ONE **sample** alert line in the
        required log file, using the exact format defined below.  
        (Because the actual load inside an automated test container is unpredictable, you
        must insert a deterministic sample alert entry yourself.)

    Required filesystem layout
    ──────────────────────────
    After you finish, the tree **must** look exactly like this (every file is mandatory):

        /home/user/monitoring/
        ├── perf_alert.sh
        ├── thresholds.conf
        └── alerts.log

    1) /home/user/monitoring/perf_alert.sh
       • POSIX shell script, first line must be “#!/bin/bash”.  
       • Must read the key-value pairs from thresholds.conf at runtime
         (do NOT hard-code the numbers in the body of the script).  
       • Must launch two background subshells in parallel:  
           a) one that samples **CPU** utilisation once per second,  
           b) another that samples **RAM** utilisation once per second.  
       • Each subshell should append to a common temporary FIFO or to
         individual temporary files and then combine the results; choose your
         own implementation, but the two samplers must run truly in parallel
         (background “&” followed by a single “wait” is sufficient).  
       • When a sample exceeds its threshold, the sampler must append ONE
         alert line to /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log following the format
         described in the “Log-file format” section.

    2) /home/user/monitoring/thresholds.conf  
       Plain text, two lines only, exactly:  
           CPU_THRESHOLD=80  
           MEM_THRESHOLD=70  

       These values must be parsed by perf_alert.sh.

    3) /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log  
       • Normal text file, created by your script if it does not exist.  
       • Must already contain one deterministic alert line that you add
         manually at the end of your session (see “Sample alert line” below).

    Log-file format (STRICT)
    ────────────────────────
    Each alert line MUST be a single line with **this exact grammar**:

        [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] ALERT: {"metric":"<cpu|memory>","value":<integer>,"threshold":<integer>}

    • The timestamp must be 24-hour format with leading zeros.  
    • The JSON fragment must not contain any extra spaces except the single
      space after the colon shown above.  
    • “metric” may only be “cpu” or “memory”.  
    • value and threshold are integers (no quotes).

    Sample alert line (must be inserted verbatim)
    ─────────────────────────────────────────────
    To guarantee the test harness can verify success even if no real
    threshold breach occurs, append the following exact line to
    /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log **after** you have verified that the
    script runs without syntax errors:

        [2023-01-01 00:00:00] ALERT: {"metric":"cpu","value":99,"threshold":80}

    (Yes, keep the historical timestamp and numbers exactly as shown.)

    Functional expectations
    -----------------------
    • Running “bash /home/user/monitoring/perf_alert.sh” in an interactive
      shell must:
        – Read thresholds.conf,
        – Spawn the two samplers in parallel,
        – Continue sampling until the user interrupts it (e.g. Ctrl-C),
        – Write any detected breaches to alerts.log immediately.

    • The script should run under ordinary user privileges and must not rely
      on systemd, sudo or cron; it should be completely self-contained.

    • Make the script executable (chmod +x).

    Verification checklist (automated tests will assert all of these)
    -----------------------------------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/monitoring exists.  
    2. File /home/user/monitoring/thresholds.conf exists and contains exactly two lines:
           CPU_THRESHOLD=80
           MEM_THRESHOLD=70
    3. File /home/user/monitoring/perf_alert.sh exists, is executable, and its first
       line is “#!/bin/bash”.  
    4. File /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log exists and contains the literal line  
       “[2023-01-01 00:00:00] ALERT: {"metric":"cpu","value":99,"threshold":80}”.
    5. When the verifier invokes “/home/user/monitoring/perf_alert.sh” and lets
       it run for at least 2 seconds, the command exits only on Ctrl-C and does
       not raise syntax errors.

    Hints (not mandatory)
    ---------------------
    • To compute CPU utilisation in shell without external packages, you can
      periodically read /proc/stat and derive the idle delta.  
    • For memory utilisation, parse “MemAvailable” and “MemTotal” in
      /proc/meminfo.  
    • Using mkfifo + tee or separate temporary files each sampled second is
      acceptable to fulfil the “parallel” requirement.

    Only the final state described above will be graded, so you may employ
    any helper commands (nano, printf, cat << EOF, etc.) while working;
    just ensure the artefacts match exactly when you are done.
truth:
<!--
        The automated checker will validate the presence and content of the
        following artefacts exactly as specified here.
    -->
    <filesystem>
        <directory path="/home/user/monitoring" />
        <file path="/home/user/monitoring/thresholds.conf">
            CPU_THRESHOLD=80
            MEM_THRESHOLD=70
        </file>
        <file path="/home/user/monitoring/perf_alert.sh">
            #!/bin/bash
            <!-- Rest of the script body can vary, but the shebang must be present.
                 The test harness will only check that the file exists, is executable,
                 contains '#!/bin/bash' on the first line, and can be launched. -->
        </file>
        <file path="/home/user/monitoring/alerts.log">
            [2023-01-01 00:00:00] ALERT: {"metric":"cpu","value":99,"threshold":80}
            <!-- The file may contain additional lines generated during the test run,
                 but the exact sample line above must appear intact. -->
        </file>
    </filesystem>
